repo_cards.description: Provides a fake file system that mocks the Python file system modules.

readme.text:
# pyfakefs [![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/pyfakefs.svg)](https://badge.fury.io/py/pyfakefs) [![Python version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/pyfakefs.svg)](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/pyfakefs.svg) ![Testsuite](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pyfakefs/workflows/Testsuite/badge.svg) [![Documentation Status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/pytest-pyfakefs/badge/?version=latest)](https://pytest-pyfakefs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest) [![pre-commit.ci status](https://results.pre-commit.ci/badge/github/pytest-dev/pyfakefs/main.svg)](https://results.pre-commit.ci/latest/github/pytest-dev/pyfakefs/main) ![PyPI - Downloads](https://img.shields.io/pypi/dw/pyfakefs)


`pyfakefs` implements a fake file system that mocks the Python file system modules.
Using `pyfakefs`, your tests operate on a fake file system in memory without
touching the real disk. The software under test requires no modification to
work with `pyfakefs`.

`pyfakefs` creates a new empty in-memory file system at each test start, which replaces
the real filesystem during the test. Think of pyfakefs as making a per-test temporary
directory, except for an entire file system.

`pyfakefs` is tested with current versions of Linux, Windows and macOS.

## Usage

There are several ways to invoke `pyfakefs`:
* using the `fs` fixture with `pytest`
* deriving from `fake_filesystem_unittest.TestCase` for `unittest`
* using `fake_filesystem_unittest.Patcher` as context manager
* using the `fake_filesystem_unittest.patchfs` decorator on a single test

Refer to the [usage documentation](https://pytest-pyfakefs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html) for more information.

## Documentation

* [Release documentation](https://pytest-pyfakefs.readthedocs.io/en/stable)
  covers the latest released version
* [Development documentation](https://pytest-pyfakefs.readthedocs.io/en/latest)
  for the current main branch
* [Release Notes](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pyfakefs/blob/main/CHANGES.md)
* [Contributing Guide](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pyfakefs/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) - contributions are welcome!

## Features
Apart from automatically mocking most file-system functions, pyfakefs
provides some additional features:
- mapping files and directories from the real file system into the fake filesystem
- configuration and tracking of the file system size
- pause and resume of patching to be able to use the real file system inside a
  test step
- (limited) emulation of other OSes (Linux, macOS or Windows)
- configuration to behave as if running as a non-root user while running
  under root

## Limitations
pyfakefs will not work with Python libraries that use C libraries to access the
file system. This is because pyfakefs cannot patch the underlying C libraries'
file access functions--the C libraries will always access the real file
system. Refer to the [documentation](https://pytest-pyfakefs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/intro.html#limitations)
for more information about the limitations of pyfakefs.

## History
pyfakefs.py was initially developed at Google by Mike Bland as a modest fake
implementation of core Python modules.  It was introduced to all of Google
in September 2006. At last count, pyfakefs was used in over 20,000
Python tests at Google.

Google released pyfakefs to the public in 2011 as a Google Code project.
Support for `unittest` and `doctest` was added in a fork by user `jmcgeheeiv`,
further corrections were made in a separate fork with user `shiffdane`, and after
the [shutdown of Google Code](http://google-opensource.blogspot.com/2015/03/farewell-to-google-code.html)
was announced, [John McGehee](https://github.com/jmcgeheeiv) merged all three Google Code projects together
[here on GitHub](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pyfakefs). In 2022, the repository has been transferred to
[pytest-dev](https://github.com/pytest-dev) to ensure continuous maintenance.

repo_cards.description: A developer CLI for working with Azure resources to build and deploy AI applications. Commands map to key workflow stages: code, build, deploy, and monitor.

readme.text:
# Azure Developer CLI (`azd`) > **From code to cloud in minutes.** A developer-centric CLI to build, deploy, and operate Azure applications. [![azd version](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https%3A%2F%2Fazuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net%2Fazd%2Fstandalone%2Flatest%2Fshield.json)](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/releases) [![VS Code Extension](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https%3A%2F%2Fazuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net%2Fazd%2Fvscode%2Flatest%2Fshield.json)](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-azuretools.azure-dev) [![GitHub Discussions](https://img.shields.io/github/discussions/Azure/azure-dev)](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/discussions) --- ## Built for you - ⚡ **Get productive fast** — Streamlined workflows to go from code to cloud in minutes - 🏗️ **Azure recommended practices built-in** — Opinionated templates that follow Azure development standards - 🧠 **Learn as you build** — Understand core Azure constructs through hands-on experience 📖 **[Get Started](https://aka.ms/azd)** · 💬 **[Join the Discussion](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/discussions)** · 📦 **[Browse Templates](https://azure.github.io/awesome-azd/)** --- ## Downloads | Artifact | Version | Download | | -------- | ------- | -------- | | CLI | ![azd version](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https%3A%2F%2Fazuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net%2Fazd%2Fstandalone%2Flatest%2Fshield.json) | [Windows](https://azuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net/azd/standalone/latest/azd-windows-amd64.zip) · [Linux](https://azuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net/azd/standalone/latest/azd-linux-amd64.tar.gz) · [macOS](https://azuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net/azd/standalone/latest/azd-darwin-amd64.zip) | | VS Code Extension | ![vscode extension version](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https%3A%2F%2Fazuresdkartifacts.z5.web.core.windows.net%2Fazd%2Fvscode%2Flatest%2Fshield.json) | [Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-azuretools.azure-dev) | ## 🤖 AI Agents **Contributing to this repo?** See [AGENTS.md](cli/azd/AGENTS.md) for coding standards and guidelines. **Using `azd` with an AI coding assistant?** Check out the [docs](https://aka.ms/azd) and [templates](https://azure.github.io/awesome-azd/). --- ## Installation Install or upgrade to the latest version. For advanced scenarios, see the [installer docs](cli/installer/README.md). ### Windows ```powershell # Using winget (recommended) winget install microsoft.azd # Or Chocolatey choco install azd # Or install script powershell -ex AllSigned -c "Invoke-RestMethod 'https://aka.ms/install-azd.ps1' | Invoke-Expression" ``` ### macOS ```bash brew install azure/azd/azd ``` > **Note:** If upgrading from a non-Homebrew installation, remove the existing `azd` binary first. ### Linux ```bash curl -fsSL https://aka.ms/install-azd.sh | bash ``` ### Shell Completion Enable tab completion for `bash`, `zsh`, `fish`, or `powershell`: ```bash azd completion --help ``` --- ## Uninstall
Windows - **v0.5.0+**: Use "Add or remove programs" or your package manager (`winget uninstall`, `choco uninstall`) - **v0.4.0 and earlier**: ```powershell powershell -ex AllSigned -c "Invoke-RestMethod 'https://aka.ms/uninstall-azd.ps1' | Invoke-Expression" ```
Linux / macOS ```bash curl -fsSL https://aka.ms/uninstall-azd.sh | bash ``` Or use your package manager's uninstall command.
--- ## Data Collection This software may collect usage data and send it to Microsoft to help improve our products. You can opt out by setting: ```bash export AZURE_DEV_COLLECT_TELEMETRY=no ``` See the [Microsoft Privacy Statement](https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=521839) for details. --- ## Contributing We welcome contributions! Please see our [contributing guide](cli/azd/CONTRIBUTING.md) for details. Most contributions require a [Contributor License Agreement (CLA)](https://cla.microsoft.com). A bot will guide you through this when you open a PR. This project follows the [Microsoft Open Source Code of Conduct](https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/). Questions? Contact [opencode@microsoft.com](mailto:opencode@microsoft.com). ### Template Authors Microsoft employees and partners contributing official templates should follow the [template standardization guidelines](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/azd-template-artifacts). --- ## Trademarks This project may contain Microsoft trademarks or logos. Use of these must follow [Microsoft's Trademark & Brand Guidelines](https://www.microsoft.com/legal/intellectualproperty/trademarks). Third-party trademarks are subject to their respective policies. ## License Licensed under the [MIT](LICENSE) license.

items.name: azure-dev-cli_1.23.14
items.body_excerpt:
## 1.23.14 (2026-04-03)

### Features Added

- [[#7489]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7489) Promote `azd update` to public preview; the command no longer requires enabling an alpha feature flag and displays a preview notice on first use.
- [[#7382]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7382) Add per-layer hook support in `azure.yaml`; hooks defined under `infra.layers[].hooks` now execute during `azd provision`, and `azd hooks run` supports a new `--layer` flag for targeted execution.
- [[#7392]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7392) Add `--non-interactive` as a global flag alias for `--no-prompt` and support the `AZD_NON_INTERACTIVE` environment variable for enabling non-interactive mode. Thanks @spboyer for the contribution!
- [[#7361]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7361) Add `docker.network` option to `azure.yaml` service configuration, passing `--network` to `docker build` for services that require host networking (e.g., behind corporate proxies). Thanks @spboyer for the contribution!
- [[#7384]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7384) `azd auth token` now prints the raw access token by default; use `--output json` for structured output including expiration metadata.
- [[#7296]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7296) `azd pipeline config` no longer prompts for parameters that are outputs of earlier provisioning layers, reducing unnecessary user prompts in multi-layer deployments.
- [[#7401]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7401) Add a "Fix this error" option to the Copilot-assisted error troubleshooting flow, allowing the agent to directly apply a fix and collect user feedback.
- [[#7397]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7397) Add `allowed_locations` filter to the `PromptLocation` extension framework API and improve AI model capacity resolution to fall back to the highest valid capacity within remaining quota.
- [[#7043]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7043) Add Key Vault secret resolver to th

items.name: azd-ext-azure-ai-agents_0.1.20-preview
items.body_excerpt:
## 0.1.20-preview (2026-04-02)

- [[#7422]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7422) Add `/invocations` API support to `azd ai agent invoke`, enabling agents to accept arbitrary input passed directly to the agent.
- [[#7341]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7341) Fix `azd ai agent init` writing unnecessary `scale` configuration for vnext-hosted agents, which is now skipped when vnext is enabled. Thanks @spboyer for the contribution!

items.name: azd-ext-azure-ai-agents_0.1.19-preview
items.body_excerpt:
## 0.1.19-preview (2026-03-31)

- [[#7327]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7327) Fix `azd ai agent init` reruns to reuse an existing azd environment instead of failing when a previous attempt already created it.
- [[#7332]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7332) Improve `azd ai agent init` performance when discovering existing Foundry projects, including faster `--project-id` validation.
- [[#7355]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7355) Update generated Application Insights environment variables to reuse the selected connection and avoid redundant connection creation during deployment.
- [[#7373]](https://github.com/Azure/azure-dev/pull/7373) Fix `postdeploy` hook failures in projects without hosted agents so unrelated azd projects no longer error during deploy.

items.title: project-id flag is ignored when agent manifest doesn't contain a model resource
Support first-class data seeding workflow in azd
Add PR governance workflow for linked issues and prioritization

scout.summary: pyfakefs is a mature Python library that provides an in-memory fake filesystem for testing, seamlessly mocking standard file system modules without requiring changes to the code under test. It integrates with pytest and unittest, supports OS emulation, and offers advanced features like real filesystem mapping and patching control. However, it has known limitations with C-based file access and occasional platform-specific bugs.
highlights: Provides a fully isolated, in-memory fake filesystem per test, avoiding side effects on the real disk [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-003].
Supports multiple integration methods: pytest fixture, unittest subclass, context manager, and decorator [ID:E-RD-004].
Offers advanced capabilities including cross-OS emulation, filesystem size tracking, and selective patching pause/resume [ID:E-RD-007].
Actively maintained with recent bugfix releases addressing Windows and test runner compatibility issues [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-RL-003].
Well-documented with separate guides for usage, releases, and contributions [ID:E-RD-005][ID:E-RD-006].
risks: Incompatible with libraries using C-level file system calls, as pyfakefs cannot intercept those [ID:E-RD-008].
Historical issues with platform-specific behavior (e.g., Windows realpath, PyPy compatibility) suggest potential edge-case fragility [ID:E-IS-001][ID:E-IS-002][ID:E-RL-002].

inventor.summary: pyfakefs invents a comprehensive, in-memory fake filesystem that transparently intercepts and mocks Python’s standard file system operations, enabling isolated, side-effect-free testing without modifying application code.
highlights: Invents a per-test, empty in-memory filesystem that fully replaces the real filesystem during test execution [ID:E-RD-003].
Enables seamless mocking of built-in file system modules (e.g., os, open) without requiring changes to the software under test [ID:E-RD-002].
Introduces flexible integration mechanisms: pytest fixture, unittest subclass, context manager, and decorator [ID:E-RD-004].
Pioneers advanced test-time controls like pausing/resuming filesystem patching and mapping real files into the fake system [ID:E-RD-007].
Implements cross-platform emulation (Linux, macOS, Windows) and user privilege simulation within the fake environment [ID:E-RD-007].
risks: The core invention cannot intercept C-level file system calls, limiting compatibility with libraries that bypass Python’s I/O stack [ID:E-RD-008].
Platform-specific behaviors (e.g., Windows realpath, PyPy runtime) reveal edge cases where the abstraction leaks, risking test reliability [ID:E-IS-001][ID:E-IS-002][ID:E-RL-002].

engineer.summary: pyfakefs is a well-architected testing utility that implements a robust in-memory fake filesystem, enabling reliable and isolated unit tests by transparently mocking Python’s file system interfaces. It supports multiple test frameworks, offers advanced features like OS emulation and patching control, and maintains active maintenance with prompt fixes for platform-specific issues—though it remains inherently limited against C-extension filesystem access.
highlights: Implements a clean, per-test in-memory filesystem that fully isolates tests from real disk side effects [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-003].
Provides four distinct integration patterns (pytest fixture, unittest subclass, context manager, decorator) for flexible adoption across test suites [ID:E-RD-004].
Includes sophisticated engineering features: real filesystem mapping, patching pause/resume, cross-OS emulation, and user privilege simulation [ID:E-RD-007].
Demonstrates responsive maintenance with recent patch releases addressing Windows path resolution and test runner compatibility [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-RL-003].
Well-structured project layout with clear documentation, contribution guidelines, and CI/CD integration (pre-commit, GitHub Actions) [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-RD-006].
risks: Fundamental architectural limitation: cannot intercept C-level file operations, breaking compatibility with libraries like those using libc I/O [ID:E-RD-008].
Recurring platform-specific bugs (e.g., Windows realpath, PyPy incompatibility) indicate ongoing challenges in maintaining cross-environment fidelity [ID:E-IS-001][ID:E-IS-002][ID:E-RL-002].

skeptic.summary: While pyfakefs offers a convenient abstraction for filesystem mocking, its reliability is undermined by recurring platform-specific bugs, fundamental limitations with C extensions, and potential overstatement of compatibility—raising concerns about test fidelity in real-world scenarios.
highlights: Claims of seamless mocking are contradicted by documented failures on PyPy and Windows, suggesting inconsistent cross-platform behavior [ID:E-IS-002][ID:E-RL-002].
The library explicitly cannot handle C-based file access, which affects common libraries (e.g., NumPy, PIL), limiting practical utility despite broad claims [ID:E-RD-008].
Recent releases are primarily bugfixes for basic functionality (e.g., os.path.realpath), indicating instability in core emulation logic [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002].
risks: Tests may pass in the fake filesystem but fail on real systems due to unemulated OS or filesystem nuances, creating false confidence [ID:E-IS-001][ID:E-RL-002].
Dependency on pure-Python I/O means integration with widely used scientific or systems libraries could silently bypass mocking, leading to accidental disk writes during tests [ID:E-RD-008].
Limited evidence of comprehensive cross-platform CI; despite OS emulation claims, bugs keep surfacing on non-Linux environments [ID:E-IS-002][ID:E-RL-002].

scout.summary: Azure/azure-dev is an active, Microsoft-backed CLI tool enabling developers to rapidly build, deploy, and manage Azure-hosted applications—particularly AI agents—with strong integration into Azure best practices and developer workflows.
highlights: Core value proposition: 'From code to cloud in minutes' with opinionated, Azure-aligned templates [E-RD-001][E-RD-003].
Actively developed with recent releases adding layer hooks, non-interactive mode, and AI agent invocation APIs [E-RL-001][E-RL-002].
Dual distribution via CLI and VS Code extension, with clear installation paths and version tracking [E-RD-002][E-RD-005].
Growing AI/agent focus evident in dedicated extensions (azd-ext-azure-ai-agents) and documentation [E-RL-002][E-RL-003][E-RD-007].
Community engagement encouraged through GitHub Discussions and contribution guidelines [E-RD-002][E-RD-006].
risks: High open issue count (504) relative to forks/stars may indicate support burden or unresolved UX gaps [repo_profile].
AI agent features still in preview (e.g., azd-ext-azure-ai-agents v0.1.x), suggesting instability or breaking changes ahead [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Some reported bugs around project-id handling and hook execution in edge cases [E-IS-001][E-RL-003].

inventor.summary: Azure/azure-dev introduces novel developer workflow abstractions for Azure, particularly through layered infrastructure hooks, AI agent integration, and opinionated deployment templates that embed cloud best practices directly into the CLI experience.
highlights: Introduces per-layer hook support in `azure.yaml`, enabling fine-grained control over provisioning workflows via `infra.layers[].hooks` [E-RL-001].
Pioneers first-class AI agent development with preview extensions (`azd-ext-azure-ai-agents`) supporting `/invocations` API and streamlined initialization [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Embeds Azure-recommended architecture patterns directly into deployable templates, turning infrastructure-as-code into a guided learning experience [E-RD-003].
Unifies developer tooling across CLI and VS Code with synchronized versioning and shared configuration (e.g., `.vscode`, `azure.yaml`) [E-RD-002][E-RF-001].
Enables non-interactive automation via global `--non-interactive` flag and `AZD_NON_INTERACTIVE` env var, improving CI/CD integration [E-RL-001].
risks: AI agent features remain in preview (v0.1.x), indicating unstable APIs and potential breaking changes that could disrupt early adopters [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Layered hook system is newly introduced; lack of documentation or examples may hinder adoption and increase misconfiguration risk [E-RL-001].
Data seeding—a critical workflow for real-world apps—is still an open feature request, forcing users to implement custom workarounds [E-IS-002].

engineer.summary: Azure/azure-dev is a Go-based CLI tool that streamlines Azure application deployment with opinionated templates, layered infrastructure hooks, and emerging AI agent support, targeting developer productivity but carrying risks from preview-stage features and unresolved workflow gaps.
highlights: Implements per-layer hook execution in `azure.yaml` for granular control during provisioning, enhancing automation flexibility [E-RL-001].
Provides global non-interactive mode via `--non-interactive` flag and `AZD_NON_INTERACTIVE` env var, critical for CI/CD integration [E-RL-001].
Extends functionality through modular extensions like `azd-ext-azure-ai-agents`, adding `/invocations` API and improved agent initialization [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Offers cross-platform CLI distribution (Windows, Linux, macOS) and VS Code extension with synchronized versioning [E-RD-005][E-RD-002].
Embeds Azure best practices directly into deployable templates, reducing onboarding friction for cloud-native development [E-RD-003].
risks: AI agent capabilities are in early preview (v0.1.x), implying unstable interfaces and potential breaking changes [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Critical data seeding workflow remains unimplemented, forcing manual workarounds for database initialization [E-IS-002].
Reported bugs in edge cases—such as `project-id` being ignored without model resources—suggest incomplete validation logic [E-IS-001].
Newly introduced layer hooks lack usage examples or documentation, increasing risk of misconfiguration [E-RL-001].

skeptic.summary: Despite its polished marketing and active development, Azure/azure-dev exhibits concerning gaps between its 'code-to-cloud-in-minutes' promise and actual maturity—particularly in AI agent workflows, data handling, and edge-case reliability.
highlights: The core claim of rapid deployment relies heavily on opinionated templates that may not reflect real-world complexity or customization needs [E-RD-001][E-RD-003].
AI agent functionality is still in early preview (v0.1.x), with recent fixes addressing basic initialization flaws—hardly production-ready [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Critical application lifecycle features like data seeding remain unimplemented after years of open requests, undermining end-to-end workflow claims [E-IS-002].
Known bugs persist in fundamental CLI behavior, such as the `project-id` flag being silently ignored under certain conditions [E-IS-001].
New capabilities like per-layer hooks are introduced without clear documentation or examples, increasing adoption risk despite their potential [E-RL-001].
risks: Overreliance on preview-stage AI features could lead to broken deployments or rework as APIs evolve [E-RL-002][E-RL-003].
Lack of data seeding support forces users into fragile, undocumented workarounds for a common requirement [E-IS-002].
High open issue count (504) suggests unresolved UX and reliability problems beneath the surface of active releases [repo_profile][E-IS-001].
Silent failures (e.g., ignored flags) indicate insufficient input validation and error feedback, eroding trust in automation [E-IS-001].
Marketing emphasizes speed and simplicity, but missing governance workflows (only recently added for PRs) hint at immature collaboration tooling [E-IS-003].
